questions.question: A sparse table's complexity profile is what?
options: Build O(n log n), query O(1). Trades preprocessing time for O(1) queries — better than segment tree when no updates needed.
questions.explanation: Sparse table precomputes range mins for all power-of-2 lengths starting at every index. The table is n × log n in size, so build is O(n log n) time and space. Then any range can be answered as min(table[k][l], table[k][r-2^k+1]) where k = floor(log2(r-l+1)). Two array lookups + one min → O(1) per query.

questions.question: Why does sparse table work for min/max/gcd but NOT for sum?
options: Sum isn't idempotent — overlapping two ranges would double-count elements in the overlap. Min/max/gcd ARE idempotent (f(x,x)=x), so overlap is harmless.
questions.explanation: Sparse table covers a range [l, r] with two POSSIBLY-OVERLAPPING power-of-2 ranges. For min: min(min[l..a], min[b..r]) where the ranges may share elements — fine because min(x, x) = x. For sum: sum[l..a] + sum[b..r] would double-count the overlap → wrong. So for sum you must use segment tree or Fenwick, which split into disjoint ranges.

questions.question: Why does the sparse-table query use floor(log2(r - l + 1))?
options: k = floor(log2(len)) gives the largest power of 2 ≤ len. The two ranges [l, l+2^k) and [r-2^k+1, r] each have length 2^k, and together cover [l, r] (with overlap). Both lookups are valid sparse-table entries.
questions.explanation: We need a power of 2 that fits in the range; the largest such is 2^floor(log2(len)). Two ranges of size 2^k starting at l and ending at r together cover the full range — they overlap in the middle if 2·2^k > len. For idempotent operations the overlap doesn't matter. Precomputing log2[i] in O(n) makes the query branchless.

questions.question: What's the memory overhead of a sparse table?
options: O(n log n) — n entries × log2(n) levels. For n=10^6, ~20 levels × 4 bytes per int = 80 MB. This is the cost of the O(1) query.
questions.explanation: Memory is the cost. For n=1M, ~20 levels × 4-8 bytes per cell = 80-160 MB. Segment tree is O(n) = 8 MB. So sparse table is best when (1) n is small to medium, (2) queries vastly outnumber memory cost, (3) data is static. For huge arrays, Bender-Farach gives O(n) preprocessing + O(1) query but with very large constants.

questions.question: Sparse table can answer LCA queries because:
options: LCA reduces to RMQ on the Euler tour (a DFS sequence with depth annotations). Sparse table on this O(n) array → O(1) LCA after O(n log n) preprocessing.
questions.explanation: Bender-Farach 2000: LCA(a, b) in a tree T = the node with minimum depth in the Euler-tour subsequence from the first occurrence of a to the first occurrence of b. The Euler tour has 2n-1 entries with depths differing by ±1. Sparse table on depths → O(1) LCA. For tree problems (compilers, type systems, network routing), this is the canonical LCA technique.

questions.question: When would you choose a segment tree over a sparse table for range-min queries?
options: When you need updates. Sparse table is static — modifying A[i] would require rebuilding the whole table O(n log n). Segment tree handles point/range updates in O(log n).
questions.explanation: Sparse table wins for static data: O(1) query vs O(log n). Segment tree wins for dynamic data: it supports updates in O(log n), sparse table does not. The decision tree: 'Will the array change after construction?' If no → sparse table (or Fischer-Heun O(n)/O(1) for huge n). If yes → segment tree (or wavelet tree for special queries).
